title: Dog Ingested Rat Poison Pellets: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your dog ate rat poison pellets, it is a high-priority emergency. Learn why immediate vet care and a photo of the packaging are critical.
content_html:
TL;DR: Rodenticide ingestion is a life-threatening medical emergency that requires immediate veterinary attention, even if your dog currently appears healthy. Always bring the product packaging or a photo of the active ingredients to the vet to ensure your dog receives the correct life-saving treatment.

What should I know about rodenticide poisoning in dogs?

Rodenticide ingestion occurs when a dog consumes toxic substances meant to kill rats or mice. These poisons usually come in pellet, block, or paste form and are often brightly colored (green, blue, or pink). They are formulated to taste good to pests, which unfortunately makes them enticing to dogs as well. Common types include anticoagulants, which prevent blood clotting, and neurotoxins like bromethalin.

Is it a medical emergency if my dog eats rat poison?

  • Yes, this is a high-priority medical emergency that requires immediate intervention.
  • Even if your dog is acting normally right now, many rodenticides have a delayed effect, causing internal damage for several days before symptoms appear.
  • Early intervention is the most effective way to prevent permanent organ damage or death.
  • You should contact a veterinarian or an animal poison control center immediately.

Why should I provide the vet with a photo of the rodenticide packaging?

  • Taking a clear photo of the product's packaging, specifically the active ingredients and the brand name, is one of the most helpful things you can do for the veterinary team.
  • Knowing exactly what your dog ate allows for a faster and more accurate treatment plan.
  • Identification is critical because different poisons require vastly different treatments, such as Vitamin K1 for anticoagulants versus specialized care for neurotoxins.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cases of suspected rodenticide ingestion in dogs, prompt action is critical. If the ingestion occurred within 4 hours, emesis can be induced using 3% hydrogen peroxide or apomorphine. For bromethalin ingestion, emesis alone may be adequate at dosages of 0.1-0.49 mg/kg in dogs; otherwise, a single dose of activated charcoal (1-2 g/kg) is indicated, keeping in mind the risk of aspiration or hypernatremia. In cases of zinc phosphide ingestion, emesis can be induced with apomorphine, and gastric acid reduction can be attempted with oral magnesium hydroxide or famotidine (1 mg/kg, SC). Baseline biochemical profiles with repeat evaluation of liver and renal values at 24, 48, and possibly 72 hours are recommended. When hemorrhage is present, differential diagnoses include disseminated intravascular coagulation, congenital factor deficiencies, von Willebrand disease, platelet deficiencies, and canine ehrlichiosis. Prolonged prothrombin time (PT), activated partial thromboplastin time (APTT), or thrombin time with normal fibrinogen, fibrin degradation products, and platelet counts strongly suggests anticoagulant rodenticide toxicosis.

Chapter: Toxicology, Hematology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 3166)

faqs.question: How soon will I see symptoms if my dog ate rat poison?
faqs.answer: Symptoms depend on the type of poison. Some cause immediate distress, while anticoagulant poisons may not show signs like bruising or bleeding for 3 to 7 days. This is why you must seek help even if they seem fine.

faqs.question: Should I induce vomiting at home?
faqs.answer: Never induce vomiting at home unless specifically instructed to do so by a licensed veterinarian. In some cases, it can cause more harm or lead to aspiration pneumonia.

faqs.question: Can a dog survive eating rat poison?
faqs.answer: Yes, with rapid veterinary treatment, the prognosis is often good. The key is removing the toxin from the system before it is fully absorbed or starting the appropriate antidote immediately.
